tiles.name: Vieux Lyon
tiles.funFact: Vieux Lyon is one of the largest Renaissance districts in Europe, spanning 424 hectares and designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1998.

tiles.name: Traboules
tiles.funFact: Lyon's traboules are secret passageways that cut through buildings and courtyards, originally used by silk workers and later by the French Resistance during World War II.

tiles.name: Lyon Part-Dieu
tiles.funFact: Lyon Part-Dieu is the largest railway station in France outside Paris, serving over 30 million passengers annually.

tiles.name: Guillotière
tiles.funFact: La Guillotière is one of Lyon's oldest neighborhoods, home to the historic Pont de la Guillotière bridge dating back to 1070.

tiles.name: Ainay
tiles.funFact: The Basilica of Saint-Martin d'Ainay in this quarter is one of the oldest churches in Lyon, consecrated by Pope Paschal II in 1107.

tiles.name: Croix-Rousse
tiles.funFact: Known as "the hill that works," Croix-Rousse was the heart of Lyon's silk weaving industry, with buildings featuring extra-tall ceilings to accommodate the Jacquard looms.

tiles.name: St-Jean
tiles.funFact: The Cathedral of Saint-Jean-Baptiste took over 300 years to build, from 1180 to 1480, and houses a 14th-century astronomical clock that still functions today.

tiles.name: EDF Lyon
tiles.funFact: Lyon's EDF operations manage electricity distribution across the Rhône-Alpes region, powering one of France's most industrialized metropolitan areas.

tiles.name: Terreaux
tiles.funFact: Place des Terreaux features the famous Bartholdi Fountain, designed by the same sculptor who created the Statue of Liberty in New York.

tiles.name: Place des Jacobins
tiles.funFact: The Place des Jacobins fountain, erected in 1885, honors four Lyonnais artists and is surrounded by elegant 19th-century Haussmann-style buildings.

tiles.name: Lyon Perrache
tiles.funFact: Opened in 1857, Gare de Perrache was Lyon's main station for over a century and sits at the southern tip of the Presqu'île peninsula.

tiles.name: Opéra
tiles.funFact: The Opéra de Lyon was radically redesigned by architect Jean Nouvel in 1993, who added a striking glass barrel-vault roof that doubled the building's height.

tiles.name: Halles de Lyon
tiles.funFact: Les Halles de Lyon Paul Bocuse is named after the legendary chef and features over 50 gourmet vendors, earning its reputation as the stomach of Lyon.

tiles.name: Presqu'île
tiles.funFact: The Presqu'île is a narrow peninsula formed between the Rhône and Saône rivers, serving as Lyon's commercial and cultural heart since Roman times.

tiles.name: Institut Lumière
tiles.funFact: The Lumière brothers filmed the world's first motion picture in Lyon in 1895, and the Institut Lumière museum occupies their family villa in the Monplaisir neighborhood.

tiles.name: Part-Dieu
tiles.funFact: The Part-Dieu district is Lyon's modern business center, dominated by the 165-meter Tour Part-Dieu skyscraper, nicknamed "the pencil" by locals for its distinctive shape.

tiles.name: Bellecour
tiles.funFact: Place Bellecour is the largest open pedestrian square in Europe, spanning 62,000 square meters, with a famous equestrian statue of Louis XIV at its center.

tiles.name: Métro A
tiles.funFact: Lyon's Métro Line A opened in 1978 and runs east-west through the city, connecting Perrache to Vaulx-en-Velin across 14 stations.

tiles.name: Grand Hôtel-Dieu
tiles.funFact: The Grand Hôtel-Dieu served as a hospital for over 800 years before being transformed into a luxury hotel, shopping, and cultural complex that reopened in 2018.

tiles.name: Confluence
tiles.funFact: Lyon Confluence is a massive urban renewal project at the meeting point of the Rhône and Saône rivers, transforming 150 hectares of former industrial land into an eco-district.

tiles.name: Veolia Lyon
tiles.funFact: Veolia manages Lyon's water supply drawn from the Rhône aquifer, delivering over 200,000 cubic meters of drinking water daily to the metropolitan area.

tiles.name: Musée des Confluences
tiles.funFact: The Musée des Confluences is housed in a futuristic deconstructivist building made of glass and steel that resembles a crystal cloud, designed by the Austrian firm Coop Himmelb(l)au.

tiles.name: Fourvière
tiles.funFact: The hill of Fourvière was the site of the original Roman settlement of Lugdunum, founded in 43 BC as the capital of the three Gauls under the Roman Empire.

tiles.name: Perrache
tiles.funFact: The Perrache quarter sits on land artificially created in the 18th century by engineer Antoine-Michel Perrache, who redirected the confluence of the two rivers southward.

tiles.name: Brotteaux
tiles.funFact: Les Brotteaux is Lyon's most upscale neighborhood, home to the stunning former Brotteaux railway station built in 1908, now converted into a gourmet food hall.

tiles.name: Métro D
tiles.funFact: Lyon's Métro Line D, opened in 1991, was the world's first fully automated driverless metro line, pioneering technology later adopted by cities worldwide.

tiles.name: Tête d'Or
tiles.funFact: Parc de la Tête d'Or is one of the largest urban parks in France at 117 hectares, featuring a free zoo, botanical garden with over 16,000 plant species, and a large lake.

tiles.name: Basilique de Fourvière
tiles.funFact: The Basilica of Notre-Dame de Fourvière was built between 1872 and 1884, and its four towers and golden Virgin Mary statue make it the most iconic landmark of the Lyon skyline.
